note: Whisper base-model source had Swiss-German recognition errors; English is intent-faithful.

segments.text: Moving from the flight to the landing — for landing it's important that beforehand
we've already worked out which direction we'll fly the landing circuit, right or left,
and above all it's important that everybody follows the same rules.
Because in the landing phase we're not far from the ground, which means we have
little or no time left to react.
Up at 1,000 metres above the ground, when something goes wrong, you can take your time
looking at the terrain and reacting. But near the landing site everything happens
much faster, simply because the ground is right there.
So the basic principle has to be that we all fly the same landing circuit, and that way
we can guarantee that we can see what the others ahead of us are doing.
The landing circuit works like this — we decide on a left-hand or right-hand circuit
and then we head to the positioning leg and into the descending circle,
which is where we lose height before entering a proper landing circuit.
And in that descending circle it's important to fly three correct, full circles.
If it's a right circuit, you fly right circles; if it's a left circuit, you fly left circles.
Then we end up at a sensible height for the landing circuit.
We teach that height during your training. After one, two, three landings
you can roughly hit the upwind leg. It varies, depending on the air mass,
on where you are — it doesn't have to be the same every time.
It can be completely different once wind is involved.
That's why it's important to develop a feel for it, and to trust that feel.
It takes everyone two, three, four flights before they get a handle on it,
and that's exactly why your instructor is with you on the radio.
I'm letting you know that very quickly you'll get the hang of it,
and after a couple of guided flights you can do it independently.
Upwind leg, crosswind leg, downwind — sorry, final leg.
When we notice we're on the upwind leg, we need clearly enough height.
Then we can comfortably turn into the crosswind leg,
and we can also extend the crosswind leg
if we have the room — fly out wider and round the corner.
If we're too short, we won't notice it quite as much,
and I can shorten it very simply, as shown here.
Or we can fly the crosswind leg with S-turns,
always following the correct reference points.
And the great thing about those S-turns is that at any moment
we can leave the crosswind leg and head into the final whenever it suits us.
We can make small course corrections ourselves
if there's a bit of sidewind, but ultimately the important thing
is to fly the target accurately.
If we notice we're far too high,
We can, for example, make ourselves big.
That means the pilot and the wing create relatively a lot of drag,
and when we sit back in the harness,
we glide well and have little drag.
Or we make ourselves bigger by sitting up out of the harness,
make sure the landing gear — your legs — is ready,
and then we generate a lot of drag,
which means we have a worse glide.
Put differently, we can lose height efficiently.
And that's how we get to our landing point.
The other option is, very gently, S-turns.
Those are allowed, too. Or, if we get there later,
We pull little-ears in — simply put, the wing becomes smaller,
we sink faster, and that also gives us a better chance
The briefing for us is that in a 30-metre circle,
if we touch down inside that circle,
Important: if we have to pull little-ears,
in that configuration we can't steer the wing very much.
but my freedom of movement is restricted,
and we need to bear that in mind.
So it's better to have the little-ears in already,
rather than doing something unconsidered or in a panic,
where the landing turns out a bit differently than we'd planned,
For example, we've flown a landing circuit.
which is always nice — that lets us land into wind,
Now we suddenly have a tailwind,
What's important is that we follow through with the landing circuit
and we just deal with landing in the tailwind.
With a tailwind we have more groundspeed in the final.
That means we have to apply the brakes faster,
than we're used to on a normal landing.
On a normal landing we go to
the brakes slowly until we're just gliding above the ground.
Landing in tailwind we need to flare crisply
where the wing stops above us and right then both feet hit the ground.
Even in tailwind you can make a clean, soft landing.
A tailwind landing is no drama, it's just a skill
I'm not talking about 30 km/h tailwind, of course,
which is something you can manage.
It can also happen that we have to do an out-landing.
The reason might be that the landing site is closed,
and we have no other option.
For an out-landing, it's important not to,
if there's a slope, not to fly into the slope.
If we're on a slope we don't fly directly into the hillside on flare,
but we land parallel to the slope.
There we make exactly the same kind of landing,
touching down parallel to the slope.
Now it can also happen that we've misjudged things
and we've overshot the landing site,
and we end up at a car park where cars are parked.
Whatever it is, there's a difference
between flying onto it at 30 km/h
and landing at only 5 km/h, or even 0,
and that way we can still land on it.
the idea is to bleed off energy and not just stay up there and shout,
Then we shouldn't worry about people watching us,
or care about being in an embarrassing situation.
It can happen. We've done something wrong,
we can't reach the landing site anymore, and we end up landing on the way to it.
but I've already chosen something better,
because a tree landing is even worse.
If you can choose, prefer the alternative to a tree,
since then I'd have a better chance,
of getting away with bumps and the like. The important thing in flight
is to bleed off energy, via the brakes,
and to protect the head — keep your head away from the branches.
Important: there's good documentation about tree rescues.
The same pilot is usually very safe in flight,
with a lot of lines and fabric around them.
The same is true for a reserve descent over a forest —
there's a lot of fabric, a lot of line.
It catches in the branches; very rarely or never does it happen
that the pilot falls through the trees to the ground.
Usually they stay hanging up there, and that's fundamentally safe.
Most accidents that happen in this situation
come from people going into shock.
When they're in shock, clearly an emergency situation,
they try to free themselves and end up falling.
Because for example you might be hanging in a tree on a steep slope
and the slope to the side is right there,
and it looks like only two metres,
but people don't notice that on the tree side
And if you unclip from the harness and fall,
Calm, calm, calm, and then take your time and say,
The smartest move, if you're in fairly civilised terrain,
is to inform the emergency services anyway.
REGA, the Swiss air rescue, can be reached almost anywhere
and they're glad to hear from you.
where you are, whether you're OK,
In my view, you need help

and getting yourself and the wing recovered with their help

In our region we're very lucky.
the rescue is set in motion very quickly.

and with your hands you can quickly get cold and stiff,
Even worse is in a river.

If the whole landing business doesn't quite go to plan,
or if someone has a landing that doesn't go quite as nicely as the K-shaped final flare
and they get injured, it's also important that we know how to look after an injured pilot.
With paragliding we unfortunately have one disadvantage: we're in a sitting position,
meaning that if we come down hard, we're sitting,
and it goes pretty directly into the spine.
That's why spinal injuries in paragliding are not uncommon.
And when we reach the injured pilot, it's really important to know that.
It means we have to calm them down first, and then we'll fairly quickly notice how badly
or where the pilot is hurt.
If it's the back and breathing still works fine,
the best thing is to leave them lying down and immediately inform REGA
so they can come in with the helicopter and pick them up.
When the helicopter comes, it's important to know it kicks up a lot of wind.
As soon as it lands — meaning, get everything packed up, maybe your harness,
maybe your wing, all the loose gear,
stash it under a stone or something so nothing flies off into the rotors and endangers the helicopter.
When the injury is to the back, it's important
that if the pilot wants to sit up or drink something — that's a normal reaction,
after an accident — we have to prevent that.
We have to stay still, not move, as long as they're responsive.
And also nothing to drink — that's also important,
because we don't know if they have internal injuries or not.
Now, now we move on to the topic of flight technique. The point is
that we should know what our wing can actually do,
or it's important what we can influence
to make the wing glide better or sink less.
And here it's quite simple — you can see it on this scale —
we get our best glide — meaning we travel from point A to point B with the most efficiency —
when we have about 5 to 15 cm of brake contact.
At that speed, we stay in the air as long as possible.
That's the minimum sink in the scale.
but per unit of time we stay aloft longest.

we're a bit above trim speed.

the slowest the paraglider will still fly,
